APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall this paper sits squarely in the scope of top NLP/ML venues: joint embedding of words, phrases and concepts from large corpora is a core representation-learning problem and the biomedical + general-entity evaluations target the right audience. The formulation and experimental protocol (adapting skip‑gram with negative sampling, multiple biomedical and Wikipedia/NYT corpora, and a novel entity similarity dataset) meet community standards, and the comparison to many baselines is strong. I downgrade from a perfect fit because the work lacks the kind of formal analysis (identifiability/convergence guarantees) and thorough reproducibility artifacts I expect — hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned — and some empirical controls/ablations (e.g., detailed component and dataset‑size sensitivity) appear limited. With those gaps addressed it would be an excellent fit. | B(4): This paper sits squarely in the representation-learning area important to top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a weakly-supervised joint embedding method for concepts/phrases/words, evaluates against strong baselines on standard biomedical benchmarks (MayoSRS, UMNSRS) and a novel real-world entity similarity dataset, and demonstrates practical applicability with distant supervision from ontologies. The empirical, application-oriented framing and dataset/resource contribution align well with the venue audience, though lack of explicit code release and missing hyperparameter reporting (and some admitted limitations on small biomedical sets) are minor concerns that keep it from being a perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall this paper sits squarely in the scope of top NLP/ML venues: joint embedding of words, phrases and concepts from large corpora is a core representation-learning problem and the biomedical + general-entity evaluations target the right audience. The formulation and experimental protocol (adapting skip‑gram with negative sampling, multiple biomedical and Wikipedia/NYT corpora, and a novel entity similarity dataset) meet community standards, and the comparison to many baselines is strong. I downgrade from a perfect fit because the work lacks the kind of formal analysis (identifiability/convergence guarantees) and thorough reproducibility artifacts I expect — hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned — and some empirical controls/ablations (e.g., detailed component and dataset‑size sensitivity) appear limited. With those gaps addressed it would be an excellent fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the representation-learning area important to top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a weakly-supervised joint embedding method for concepts/phrases/words, evaluates against strong baselines on standard biomedical benchmarks (MayoSRS, UMNSRS) and a novel real-world entity similarity dataset, and demonstrates practical applicability with distant supervision from ontologies. The empirical, application-oriented framing and dataset/resource contribution align well with the venue audience, though lack of explicit code release and missing hyperparameter reporting (and some admitted limitations on small biomedical sets) are minor concerns that keep it from being a perfect fit.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a fairly broad empirical evaluation across multiple corpora (PubMed, clinical, Wiki/NYT) and many competitive baselines, and even introduces a new entity similarity dataset — so experiments are reasonably extensive. However, from the summary there is no theoretical analysis or identifiability guarantees (which I heavily weight), ablation/sensitivity studies and hyperparameter/training details are missing, and code/hyperparameters are not reported, harming reproducibility. The authors do discuss some limitations (ontology structure and dataset-size effects) but fail to provide the controlled analyses (per-component ablations, dataset-size scaling, supervised-oracle comparisons) I expect to justify when and why distant supervision recovers correct concept embeddings. | B(3): The paper provides reasonably comprehensive empirical comparisons across multiple corpora (PubMed, clinical notes, Wikipedia+NYT) and a wide set of baselines, and even introduces a new real‑world entity similarity/relatedness dataset — all positives from an application perspective. However, the work appears to lack ablation/sensitivity analyses (no hyperparameter reporting) and does not mention code or pretrained embeddings release, which weakens reproducibility and makes it hard to assess which components drive gains. Related work coverage is thorough and the authors do discuss limitations and some negative results (e.g., being outperformed on smaller biomedical data), but the missing experimental details and ablations justify a middle score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a fairly broad empirical evaluation across multiple corpora (PubMed, clinical, Wiki/NYT) and many competitive baselines, and even introduces a new entity similarity dataset — so experiments are reasonably extensive. However, from the summary there is no theoretical analysis or identifiability guarantees (which I heavily weight), ablation/sensitivity studies and hyperparameter/training details are missing, and code/hyperparameters are not reported, harming reproducibility. The authors do discuss some limitations (ontology structure and dataset-size effects) but fail to provide the controlled analyses (per-component ablations, dataset-size scaling, supervised-oracle comparisons) I expect to justify when and why distant supervision recovers correct concept embeddings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides reasonably comprehensive empirical comparisons across multiple corpora (PubMed, clinical notes, Wikipedia+NYT) and a wide set of baselines, and even introduces a new real‑world entity similarity/relatedness dataset — all positives from an application perspective. However, the work appears to lack ablation/sensitivity analyses (no hyperparameter reporting) and does not mention code or pretrained embeddings release, which weakens reproducibility and makes it hard to assess which components drive gains. Related work coverage is thorough and the authors do discuss limitations and some negative results (e.g., being outperformed on smaller biomedical data), but the missing experimental details and ablations justify a middle score.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of prior methods (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, APPROXPHRco/WORD, PHRASEco) and evaluates with standard metrics (Spearman, cosine, MAP) on multiple biomedical and general-domain datasets, which meets a high bar for baseline coverage. However, I downgrade one point because training details and hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned in the summary, making it difficult to ensure full experimental fairness and reproducibility; while the authors do acknowledge when prior work (e.g., CHOI) outperforms them on small biomedical data, the paper does not clearly present thorough per-component analyses that isolate why gains arise relative to each strong baseline. | B(4): The paper compares against a wide set of relevant baselines (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, WORD/PHRASE baselines) and evaluates on multiple standard biomedical benchmarks plus a new entity similarity dataset, which I value. Comparisons appear mostly fair (same metrics like Spearman's rho, MAP) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods (e.g., CHOI on smaller biomedical data) outperform their approach. However, reporting gaps (no mention of code release or hyperparameter details) and limited discussion of some practical differences in training conditions leave minor concerns about full reproducibility and absolute fairness. Overall the empirical comparison is strong with only modest omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of prior methods (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, APPROXPHRco/WORD, PHRASEco) and evaluates with standard metrics (Spearman, cosine, MAP) on multiple biomedical and general-domain datasets, which meets a high bar for baseline coverage. However, I downgrade one point because training details and hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned in the summary, making it difficult to ensure full experimental fairness and reproducibility; while the authors do acknowledge when prior work (e.g., CHOI) outperforms them on small biomedical data, the paper does not clearly present thorough per-component analyses that isolate why gains arise relative to each strong baseline.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a wide set of relevant baselines (e.g., DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU variants, WORD/PHRASE baselines) and evaluates on multiple standard biomedical benchmarks plus a new entity similarity dataset, which I value. Comparisons appear mostly fair (same metrics like Spearman's rho, MAP) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods (e.g., CHOI on smaller biomedical data) outperform their approach. However, reporting gaps (no mention of code release or hyperparameter details) and limited discussion of some practical differences in training conditions leave minor concerns about full reproducibility and absolute fairness. Overall the empirical comparison is strong with only modest omissions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: contributions are stated early, the method section follows logically from the introduction, and experiments and conclusion are placed in the expected order. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative; the writing quality is described as good. As a theory-focused reader I note minor presentation gaps relevant to reproducibility—hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned—which prevent a top score despite generally clear exposition. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, with main contributions stated early (method, distant supervision idea, and new dataset). Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures/tables and examples are described as effective. Writing quality is good overall, though presentation would benefit from reporting hyperparameters and an explicit code/dataset release statement to improve reproducibility and clarity for practitioners like me.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and readable: contributions are stated early, the method section follows logically from the introduction, and experiments and conclusion are placed in the expected order. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative; the writing quality is described as good. As a theory-focused reader I note minor presentation gaps relevant to reproducibility—hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned—which prevent a top score despite generally clear exposition.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, with main contributions stated early (method, distant supervision idea, and new dataset). Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures/tables and examples are described as effective. Writing quality is good overall, though presentation would benefit from reporting hyperparameters and an explicit code/dataset release statement to improve reproducibility and clarity for practitioners like me.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and provides useful empirical results plus a novel dataset, making it directly valuable to practitioners. The dissent centers on limited theoretical analysis and incomplete reproducibility artifacts; these are real limitations but do not outweigh the clear practical utility and likely follow-up work enabled by the approach, so a score of 4 (strong impact but not paradigm-shifting) is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words without human annotations—and shows competitive empirical results on biomedical and general-entity tasks while contributing a novel similarity/relatedness dataset. However, from a theory-focused perspective its impact is limited: there is no formal analysis of identifiability or conditions under which distant supervision recovers true concept embeddings, hyperparameters and code are not reported (hindering reproducibility), and downstream utility and sensitivity analyses appear limited. Given solid empirical comparisons to many baselines but lacking theoretical guarantees and full reproducibility artifacts, I judge the potential impact as moderate rather than field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words without manual annotations—by exploiting ontology-derived phrase forms as distant supervision, which substantially lowers annotation burden and aligns with my preference for weak supervision. Empirically competitive biomedical concept embeddings and the release of a novel real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset make the work directly useful to practitioners and likely to be built upon, especially for downstream entity-centric applications. While not fully transformative (some limitations on abstract concepts, smaller-data settings, and no explicit code/hyperparameter release noted), it opens clear research directions for integrating ontology structure and expanding to general-domain knowledge, so I judge the impact as strong but not paradigm-shifting.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a practical and useful adaptation of skip‑gram/negative sampling to jointly embed words, phrases, and ontology‑linked concepts, and that the new dataset and weakly‑supervised framing add clear community value. While the approach builds on well‑known components and lacks deep theoretical novelty or guarantees, the pragmatic formulation and dataset represent a non‑trivial, broadly applicable contribution rather than a merely incremental engineering tweak, so a score slightly above mid‑range is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a practical and useful adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling to jointly embed words, phrases, and ontology-linked concepts using ontology-derived phrase forms as distant supervision — a sensible methodological extension and a valuable dataset contribution. However, this is largely an engineering-level augmentation of well-established distributional embedding techniques (word2vec variants) rather than a fundamentally new theoretical approach; no formal analysis or identifiability guarantees are provided. The manuscript clearly differentiates itself from prior work and evaluates against many baselines, but the core technique is a non-trivial yet incremental extension rather than a breakthrough that would surprise an expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practical novelty: reframing concept/phrase embedding as a weakly‑supervised joint training problem that uses ontology‑derived phrase forms as distant supervision to learn concept embeddings from raw text without human annotation. The methodological change is a pragmatic, non‑trivial adaptation of skip‑gram/negative sampling (including normalization for ambiguous phrases) rather than a wholly new learning paradigm, so it is more than incremental but not radical. The authors also contribute a new general‑domain entity similarity/relatedness dataset, which strengthens the originality and utility for the community. Given these facts, the work is a clear and useful extension of prior methods with distinct framing, though it builds on well‑known components rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a clear claim and is empirically well-supported across multiple benchmarks, but lacks theoretical guarantees and important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, training specifics, code). The method’s straightforward technical correctness and broad experimental validation justify a positive score, while missing reproducibility information and limited analysis of failure cases (especially on smaller biomedical corpora) warrant a modest deduction.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides extensive empirical comparisons across multiple datasets and many prior baselines, but it lacks formal analysis or identifiability guarantees for when distant supervision recovers true concept embeddings. Mathematical treatment appears lightweight (no proofs) and key reproducibility details (hyperparameters, negative sampling rates, training schedules, and code release) are not reported, which hampers verification. Experimental methodology is reasonably broad in baselines but omits explicit supervised-oracle comparisons and statistical significance testing, and admits the method is outperformed on smaller biomedical data—indicating limitations not fully explained theoretically. Overall the results are promising but technical gaps in theory, ablations/hyperparameter sensitivity, and reproducibility justify a cautious, middle score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states and experimentally supports its main claim that ontology-derived phrase forms can provide effective distant supervision to jointly learn concept/phrase/word embeddings, with competitive results across multiple biomedical and general-domain benchmarks and reasonable baselines. The technical approach (skip-gram adaptation with negative sampling and normalization for ambiguity) is straightforward and appears correct; no complex proofs were required and none seem incorrect. Concerns that keep this from a perfect score: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which hinders reproducibility, and the method is explicitly outperformed on smaller biomedical corpora (CHOI), suggesting limits to robustness that are not fully analyzed. Overall the experiments and comparisons are sound, but missing reproducibility details and limited failure analysis justify a minor deduction.

main_claims: Proposes a weakly-supervised method to jointly embed concepts, phrases, and words into a shared vector space using ontology-derived phrase forms as distant supervision.
The method requires no human corpus annotations and trains concept embeddings from contexts of their phrase mentions in raw text.

Learned embeddings for biomedical concepts and real-world entities are competitive with prior models that used human annotations, and the ontology structure is reflected in the embedding organization.
Presents a novel dataset of similarity and relatedness judgments for real-world entities identified by Wikipedia page and text phrase.
_summary.methodology: Adapts the skip-gram with negative sampling (word2vec) to jointly train word (EW), phrase (EP), and concept (EC) embeddings by treating known phrase occurrences as distant supervision for their mapped concepts; training maximizes context likelihood and uses negative sampling over tagged and untagged corpora with normalization over ambiguous phrases.
datasets: CLINICAL (Ohio State Wexner Medical Center clinical notes)
Novel general-domain real-world entity similarity and relatedness dataset
limitations: Some non-lexical information about concepts is not being captured by the method and may require including ontology structure directly.
Method may benefit from application to more abstract general-domain data.
Optimized hyperparameters for the intrinsic evaluations may change for downstream tasks.
On smaller biomedical datasets the method is outperformed by CHOI (prior work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - representation learning / embeddings for concepts and entities

reviewer_a.name: Theory-Focused Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and NLP with a track record of work on theoretical foundations of representation learning and algorithmic guarantees.
traits: Downgrades papers that lack formal analysis: penalizes submissions without proofs or theoretical guarantees about model identifiability, convergence, or when/why distant supervision is expected to recover true concept embeddings.
Requires strong controlled experiments: subtracts points if ablation studies are missing or limited (expects per-component ablations, hyperparameter sensitivity, and dataset-size scaling analyses).
Demands comprehensive baselines: scores lower if fewer than three competitive baselines are compared, including at least one supervised oracle and one prior weakly-supervised method.

Penalizes unclear reproducibility: deducts points when training details (negative sampling rates, optimization schedule, ambiguous-phrase normalization formula) and code/data are not fully specified or not released.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, conservative reviewer who assigns high scores only when a paper supplies formal motivation plus comprehensive, controlled empirical analyses and clear reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in applied NLP with experience deploying representation models for biomedical and web-scale entity understanding and creating evaluation datasets.
traits: Rewards novel practical problem framing and resources: gives credit for new datasets or evaluation suites (expects clear annotation protocols but is forgiving of initial dataset imperfections).
Favors empirical effectiveness and applicability: prioritizes papers that achieve competitive or superior performance on downstream or domain-relevant tasks even without formal proofs.

Accepts distant/weak supervision: positively biases toward methods that reduce annotation cost by exploiting ontologies or noisy labels, provided empirical results are convincing and comparisons to baselines are plausible.
Values code and dataset release: increases score if authors commit to releasing pretrained embeddings and the new similarity/relatedness dataset; otherwise deducts moderately.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic, application-oriented reviewer who rewards empirical impact, new datasets, and methods that lower annotation burden even when theoretical analysis is limited.
